one_line_takeaway: A natively-streaming output moderator (SCM) plus a token-labeled dataset (FineHarm) that stops harmful LLM output after ~18% of tokens on average while retaining full-detection-level accuracy (macro F1 95-98).
key_finding: SCM matches full-detection accuracy while seeing far fewer tokens. SCM macro F1: 95.64 (0.5B), 97.91 (1.5B), 97.45 (7B) — comparable to or better than the strong full-detection ToxDectRoBERTa (96.85) and ModernBERT (96.44), and far above off-the-shelf full-detection APIs (Perspective 75.50, OpenAI Moderation 76.51, LlamaGuard3-8B 90.79). Crucially, naively applying Qwen2.5 to incomplete input (partial via full paradigm) collapses (e.g. Qwen2.5-7B partial macro F1 78.60 vs SCM-7B 97.45, +18.85), demonstrating the training-inference gap the paper targets. Timeliness: ~50% of harmful responses stopped within the first 10% of tokens, >80% within 30%, 18% on average. Ablation shows the logic-consistency loss balances missing-alarm vs false-alarm rates. Secondary result: using SCM token scores as process supervision for token-level DPO (TokenDPO) raises harmlessness scores over plain DPO on PKU-SafeRLHF (judged by GPT-4.1) while keeping MT-Bench helpfulness comparable.
core_contribution: The paper proposes a data-and-model solution for 'native partial detection' of harmful LLM output, i.e. a moderator that inspects the LLM's output stream token-by-token and stops generation midway once enough harmful tokens are detected, instead of waiting for the complete response. Two concrete artifacts are the paper's own contribution: (1) FineHarm, a 29K prompt-response dataset with fine-grained token-level harmfulness labels produced by a POS-based heuristic; and (2) the Streaming Content Monitor (SCM), a plug-and-play monitor (built on Qwen2.5 backbones) trained with a 'hierarchical consistency-aware learning' strategy that injects response-level (holistic) knowledge into token-level judgments so the monitor can predict eventual harmfulness from incomplete text. The key claimed distinction from prior partial-detection tools (ProtectAI, GuardrailsAI) is that those reuse moderators trained under the full-detection paradigm on incomplete inputs, creating a training-inference gap that delays stopping; SCM is trained natively on incomplete/token-level supervision.
threat_model: External output-side content moderation. Even safety-aligned LLMs may emit harmful content (whether elicited by jailbreak/malicious prompts or benign prompts), and intrinsic alignment trades off harmlessness vs. helpfulness and can be broken. LLM service providers therefore deploy a second guardrail that inspects generated output before it reaches the user. Conventional 'full detection' moderators (Perspective, OpenAI Moderation, LlamaGuard) judge only the complete output, so harmful text either leaks to the user before withdrawal or adds latency. The adversary/failure mode SCM addresses is harmful tokens being exposed to the user during streaming generation; SCM's goal is to terminate generation as early as possible (low missing-alarm rate) while keeping false alarms controllable, with a tunable Delay-k knob so providers set sensitivity per scenario (e.g. small k for children's content, larger k for customer service UX).
limitations: Author-stated: (1) the POS-based heuristic annotation may miss complex semantic/pragmatic harm cues; (2) SCM performance depends on training-data coverage/representativeness and may miss unseen harm types or over-flag; (3) the safety-alignment (TokenDPO) application is preliminary and not extensively validated. Reviewer-noted: no publicly available partial-detection baseline exists, so the paper builds its own Qwen2.5 partial-detection baselines — the comparison is against self-constructed contrasts rather than external streaming moderators; harmful-response labels themselves rest on majority voting of three imperfect APIs, so 'ground truth' is model-derived; the safety-alignment table (Table 4) uses a single uncensored base model and GPT-4.1 as sole judge, and some harmlessness sub-scores are mixed (Physical harm drops under TokenDPO vs DPO).

datasets_used: FineHarm (paper's own; 29K prompt-response pairs with token-level labels; coarse pool 14,064 harmful + 29,564 benign responses; 8:1:1 train/val/test split)
WildGuard (source of 8,338 harmful + 29,564 benign responses spanning 9 LLMs)
WildJailbreak (prompts for generating additional harmful responses via uncensored LLM)
MT-Bench (helpfulness evaluation for the alignment experiment)
PKU-SafeRLHF (harmlessness evaluation for the alignment experiment)
quantitative_results: Absolute macro-F1 gain of SCM over Qwen2.5 partial detection at same scale: +6.87 (0.5B), +10.12 (1.5B), +18.85 (7B)
Timeliness: ~50% of harmful responses detected within first 10% of tokens; >80% within first 30%; 18% of tokens on average
Detection latency (avg ms, SCM): 0.28 (0.5B), 0.34 (1.5B), 1.22 (7B), each below the corresponding Qwen2.5 generation latency (0.32/0.38/1.25)
POS annotation strategy macro F1 97.91 vs alternatives Direct 95.38, Diff 89.92, Delete 92.76 (Table 5)

TokenDPO PKU-SafeRLHF harmlessness (GPT-4.1 judge) beats DPO on Social bias (5.64 vs 5.57), Pornographic (5.11 vs 4.11), Emotional harm (7.20 vs 2.60), Insult (7.60 vs 6.50); Physical harm lower (3.90 vs 5.70)
baselines_compared: Full detection: ModernBERT-large 0.4B (96.44); ModernBERT partial variant (93.21)
Qwen2.5 0.5B/1.5B/7B full and partial detection (self-constructed contrasts to SCM)
Safety alignment: DPO vs Token-level DPO on Llama-3.1-8B-Uncensored
